critic_raw:
This design uses a multi-modal neural network (genomic + tabular + clinical notes via BioBERT) versus a genomic-only baseline to predict immunotherapy response in 500 melanoma patients.

The dataset contains 500 patients split into 300 train / 100 validation / 100 test. With approximately 20–40% response rate in melanoma immunotherapy, the test set contains roughly 20–40 responders. AUROC computed from bootstrap on 100 patients with this class distribution will have confidence intervals spanning 0.10–0.15 AUROC units, meaning meaningful differences between models will be statistically undetectable. More critically, the multi-modal model has three branches and a fusion layer — substantially more parameters than the genomic-only baseline. Training a higher-capacity model on 300 patients with 60–120 positive examples is a high-risk overfitting scenario that neither early stopping nor dropout reliably resolves at this scale. The expected result is that the multi-modal model overfits to spurious correlations in clinical notes and shows inflated validation performance but does not generalize to the 100-patient test set.

A stratified random split is used rather than a temporal split, with justification that 'temporal ordering reflects documentation sequence, not predictive information flow.' However, in a clinical cancer cohort, temporal ordering carries real information: treatment protocols evolve, reporting quality changes, and referring physician practices shift over time. A stratified random split allows the model to train on later-period data and test on earlier-period data, which is the reverse of deployment. More problematically, in a multi-site consortium, patients from the same site may be allocated across train/val/test, meaning the model may learn site-specific documentation patterns from test-set patients' 'siblings' in the training set.

BioBERT is described as 'not retrained on this cohort to avoid label leakage.' This is a reasonable decision, but the clinical notes are pathology reports and treatment summaries. Pathology reports written at diagnosis may contain information about staging, margins, and other features that directly predict response — and this information may also appear in the tabular clinical features (stage, TMB). The note embeddings thus partially duplicate structured features while adding noise from unstructured language. The fusion network must learn to de-duplicate this information, which is a non-trivial learning problem with 300 training examples.
all_issues_raised: With 500 patients and a ~20-40% response rate, the 100-patient test set contains too few responders to produce statistically meaningful AUROC comparisons; confidence intervals will span 0.10-0.15 AUROC units.

The multi-modal model has substantially more parameters than the genomic-only baseline; training a higher-capacity model on 300 patients with 60-120 positive examples creates high overfitting risk that the evaluation cannot reliably detect.

Stratified random splitting in a multi-site cohort allows the model to train on patients from the same institution as test-set patients, learning site-specific documentation patterns that inflate apparent out-of-sample performance.

Pathology reports and treatment summaries in clinical notes overlap substantially with tabular clinical features (stage, TMB, prior therapy); the fusion network must de-duplicate correlated information with only 300 training examples, an underconstrained learning problem.
all_issues_adjudicated: With 500 patients and a ~20-40% response rate, the 100-patient test set contains too few responders to produce statistically meaningful AUROC comparisons; confidence intervals will span 0.10-0.15 AUROC units.

The multi-modal model has substantially more parameters than the genomic-only baseline; training a higher-capacity model on 300 patients with 60-120 positive examples creates high overfitting risk that the evaluation cannot reliably detect.

Stratified random splitting in a multi-site cohort allows the model to train on patients from the same institution as test-set patients, learning site-specific documentation patterns that inflate apparent out-of-sample performance.
